coredata.dc:title: A modelling approach for laser welding of busbars to lithium-ion prismatic cell terminals to enhance failure prediction

Beaumont, Richard
Sanguesa Gomez, Javier
Harris, Christopher
Masters, Iain
coredata.dc:description: Laser welding is increasingly being used to connect automotive battery packs and weld busbars to prismatic cell terminals due to its ability to weld a wide variety of materials at high speed. The choice of material for the connecting busbars is primarily based on weldability, weight, electrical/thermal conductivity and cost. Aluminum (Al) is increasingly being chosen as busbar material to meet these criteria. This paper investigates laser overlap welding of Al for producing busbar-to-terminal interconnects for prismatic cell assembly. To understand the strength of these laser-welded joints in the early stages of design, the joints must be modelled. In addition, the structural modelling of battery module connections is critical to understanding the impact resistance (crash safety) of electric vehicles. Fusion-based modelling of laser welding is computationally inefficient and difficult to integrate into large structural models e.g., full vehicle crash models. Instead, it is more computationally efficient to generate a structural model of the weld, tuned to experimental test data, which can be easily integrated into larger models. LS-DYNA was employed to model the mechanical behavior of 1 mm to 1 mm AA1050 laser welds. Welding was performed using a wobble head integrated with a 1 kW CW fiber laser system. The simulation is based on laser weld with a rectangular pattern and was developed from experimental data from lap shear, T-peel, cross-tension and torsion tests. This modelling approach was extended to obtain joint strength for laser welding a 1.5 mm 1060 Al busbar to a 4 mm 1060 Al prismatic cell terminal. The predicted load–displacement curves closely matched the test data.

head.ce:title: A modelling approach for laser welding of busbars to lithium-ion prismatic cell terminals to enhance failure prediction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Laser welding is increasingly being used to connect automotive battery packs and weld busbars to prismatic cell terminals due to its ability to weld a wide variety of materials at high speed. The choice of material for the connecting busbars is primarily based on weldability, weight, electrical/thermal conductivity and cost. Aluminum (Al) is increasingly being chosen as busbar material to meet these criteria. This paper investigates laser overlap welding of Al for producing busbar-to-terminal interconnects for prismatic cell assembly. To understand the strength of these laser-welded joints in the early stages of design, the joints must be modelled. In addition, the structural modelling of battery module connections is critical to understanding the impact resistance (crash safety) of electric vehicles. Fusion-based modelling of laser welding is computationally inefficient and difficult to integrate into large structural models e.g., full vehicle crash models. Instead, it is more computationally efficient to generate a structural model of the weld, tuned to experimental test data, which can be easily integrated into larger models. LS-DYNA was employed to model the mechanical behavior of 1 mm to 1 mm AA1050 laser welds. Welding was performed using a wobble head integrated with a 1 kW CW fiber laser system. The simulation is based on laser weld with a rectangular pattern and was developed from experimental data from lap shear, T-peel, cross-tension and torsion tests. This modelling approach was extended to obtain joint strength for laser welding a 1.5 mm 1060 Al busbar to a 4 mm 1060 Al prismatic cell terminal. The predicted load–displacement curves closely matched the test data.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion (Li-ion) battery technology is widely used for powering electric vehicles (EVs) [1] due to its high energy density, long cycle life, and excellent performance characteristics [2,3]. Prismatic cells (Fig. 1 a) are often used by OEMs such as Jaguar Land Rover, General Motors, Daimler and Geely in the manufacturing of battery packs [4]. The SVolt 86 Ahr prismatic cell was selected for the ASTIR project as was representative of the cell construction (Al casing + stacked ‘jelly roll’) and chemistry (LMC) intended to be used by JLR in the future. As the battery terminal and busbar are made of aluminum (Al) alloy due to their excellent mechanical properties, low density, good corrosion resistance, low cost, lightweight, high thermal conductivity and ease of formability/manufacturability [5]. In general, EV battery structures are composed of several Li-ion cells, which are welded in series and/or in parallel, to form the battery packs [6–8]. To connect the individual cells the busbar needs to be connected to the positive/negative terminals of the cell utilizing some joining technique, e.g., ultrasonic welding, resistance spot welding and laser beam welding [8–10]. Fig. 1b shows a schematic of prismatic Li-ion cells interconnected with busbar using laser-welded busbar-to-cell joints. Among the different welding technologies, laser welding is an increasingly used joining technology that has shown excellent potential in producing cell-to-busbar joints [11,12]. Even for dissimilar materials, laser welding can obtain joints with high joint strength and low electrical resistance [13,14]. However, the major challenges during laser welding aluminum alloys were observed to be the formation of porosities which decreased the joint strength and other defects such as cracks and fatigue failure [15–17]. In the research of Tao and Yang [18] and Fetzer et al. [19], the amount of porosity in Al lap welds was significantly reduced by using circular laser beam wobbling. In addition, the laser beam wobbling process has been used by several researchers in order to increase the contact area between cell terminal and busbar, as well as to keep the welding depth at a non-critical level [20–22]. For example, in the work done by Das et al. [23], it was shown, for Al-to-Al overlap joints, that the aspect ratio of the weld zone is dependent on the wobble parameters (i.e., wobble amplitude and frequency). In [24], it claimed that the laser beam wobbling can increase the fraction of equiaxed grains within the weld, thus enhancing the joint ductility of Al joints. It's worth mentioning that laser beam wobbling technique could improve Al alloy weld morphology [23,25–27].

In addition, this recent uptake of EVs demands a significant level of research and development in the fields of battery modelling and simulation of mechanical abuse e.g., as the result of a collision when a battery is subjected to large forces as shown in Fig. 1c and d, i.e., in-plane and out-of-plane indentation. The busbar and terminal joints are an integral part of the battery module together with the cells and ancillary components. To predict mechanical responses of Li-ion battery cells under mechanical abuse, structural finite element (FE) models are being developed to represent the battery cells, battery modules, and subsequently the pack behaviour at micro and/or macro levels [28–35]. However, battery interconnects are not extensively considered within these FE models. Basically, their models are primarily focused on jellyrolls and cell casings. Therefore, battery interconnect models integrated with the module model are necessary to replicate the accurate behaviour of battery modules. Some finite element models related to the laser welding of Al sheets have been introduced in previous publications [36–44], but mainly focus on the estimation of many important weld parameters, including fusion zone geometry, temperature gradient, stress distribution and thermo-mechanical distortion. Such FE weld pool geometry-based models or heat sources-based models are difficult to integrate into a complete battery pack model. Additionally, these detailed models are considered expensive and not suitable for integration with large models such as vehicle bodies or EV battery module/pack collision/crash simulations as shown in Fig. 1c and d. To solve this problem, a computationally inexpensive model is needed, which should offer good solutions for implementing FE analysis for vehicle applications. Kuppuswamy et al. [45] developed a coarse FE model to estimate the impact strength of body-in-white (BIW) laser joints. They developed a substitute simplified FE model consisting of the upper and lower flanges modelled using shell elements and a solid weld element between the flanges. The element size used in the flanges is similar to that used in the BIW structure. Chauffray et al. [46] and Arif [47] developed a numerical model for the prediction of laser welded joint failure in vehicle seat mechanisms. They obtained the strength of the laser welds and subsequent failure prediction. The same approach was adopted by Das et al. [21] to model the tab (0.3 mm copper) to cylindrical cell terminal (0.3 mm Hilumin) laser welds for EVs battery interconnections. In this study, a similar modelling approach is adopted to model the prismatic cell interconnects for easy integration with larger simulation models. In addition, it is possible to see a fracture of the joint between the busbar and terminal in the battery pack as a result of the crash as shown in Fig. 1e, which can also be a source of electrical failure and thermal runaway. The recent progress and limitations in modelling battery modules under mechanical abuse, as mentioned earlier, have emphasized the significance of creating dedicated FE models for busbar-to-cell terminal joints.

The aforesaid research gap is addressed in this paper in two stages. Firstly, laser weld models were developed for 1 mm to 1 mm AA1050 and validated against experimental test results. Secondly, the same modelling approach was adopted to model the actual busbar to prismatic cell terminal laser welded joints in support of battery assemblies.
This section presents the procedures for test sample preparation and welding process parameter determination. The mechanical and metallurgical evaluation processes are also explained.

The busbar and cell terminal thicknesses of approximately 1.5 mm and 4 mm, respectively. In general, the cell terminals and busbars were made of 1060 Al grade due to their high electrical conductivity, good corrosion resistance, weldability and formability [48]. The chemical compositions (wt%) of 1060 Al were 99.60 % Al, 0.14 % Fe, 0.12 % Si, 0.03 % Cu, 0.03 % V, 0.02 % Mg, 0.01 % Mn, 0.03 % Zn and 0.02 % Ti [49]. To create the representative busbar to prismatic cell terminal connections, laser welding was performed with the 1.5 mm 1060 Al sheet placed on top of the 4 mm 1060 Al sheet. As a preliminary step prior to laser welding and modelling of the actual busbar and cell terminal materials, trials were carried out on different grades and thicknesses of Al materials to determine the capability of the model to represent accurately the experimental results and to validate the modelling process. 1 mm thick AA1050 sheets were selected for the trials and samples prepared in lap shear, T-peel, cross-tension and torsion configurations with a 6 mm × 4 mm rectangular seam weld placed in the overlap region. Schematics of the specimens including dimensions are shown in Fig. 2 . The chemical compositions (wt%) of the AA1050 were 99.42 % Al, 0.28 % Fe, 0.11 % Si, 0.08 % Cu, 0.006 % Mg, 0.02 % Mn, 0.01 % Zn and 0.01 % Ti [50]. The stress–strain behaviour of the parent materials was determined using an 80 mm gauge length sample tested at a quasi-static speed of 0.5 mm/min. The geometry of the test pieces and the stress–strain curves of 1 mm AA1050, 1.5 mm and 4 mm 1060 Al base materials are given in Fig. 3 a and b, respectively. The physical and mechanical properties of both 1050 and 1060 Al are given in Table 1 obtained from the tests.

Welding of 1 mm AA1050 sheets as per joint configuration given in Fig. 2 was conducted using a 1 kW YLR continuous-mode (CW) fibre laser (make: IPG Photonics; model: MultiAxis SYS-MA-YLR Workstation) with 0.28 µm spot diameter [5] as shown in Fig. 4 a. Further, a clamping device was used to maintain a zero gap between the two sheets as shown in Fig. 4b. Finally, the welding was conducted using laser beam wobble techniques (i.e., circular clockwise) as shown in Fig. 4c. An argon gas jet emerging at a flow rate of 20 L/min [5] from a nozzle coaxial with the laser beam was used to avoid any external atmospheric contamination during welding. For laser welding of AA1050, Kumar et al. [5] and Das et al. [23] also used argon flow rates of 20 L/min as an optimized flow rate in order to obtain a high-quality weld.

The welding parameters and their limits are given in Table 2 . The experiments were carried out keeping the welding speed, wobble frequency and wobble amplitude constant whilst the laser power was varied to avoid overheating during welding [8]. Das et al. [23], studied the effect of laser power (P), welding speed (S), wobble amplitude (WA) and wobble frequency (Wf) on the laser wobble welding of AA1050. They found that the P had the most significant influence on the two key geometric features of the fusion zone, i.e., weld width (WW) and depth of penetration (DOP). Therefore, based on the joint strength obtained in the pilot study, the WA, Wf and S were determined as 0.5 mm, 600 Hz and 2000 mm/min, respectively, and then fixed. The process parameters were only optimised for the lap joint, and the determined parameters used other joint configurations i.e., T-peel, cross-tension and torsion. Furthermore, welding of actual busbars to cell terminal and representative coupon materials was conducted with a 4 kW single mode CW laser (make: IPG Photonics; model: YLS-4000-CT) integrated with a robotic control system.

After welding, the joint strength of the welded samples was measured using an Instron 3367 test frame at a cross-head speed of 0.5 mm/min. The load vs. displacement curves from lap shear, T-peel and cross-tensile tests were recorded to evaluate the mechanical strength of the weld and were also used for model validation. In addition, the torque-rotation angle curves from the torsion tests using a Zwick 250 were recorded and then used for model validation. The welded samples for metallographic analysis were sectioned perpendicular to the welding direction. The weld bead geometry was analysed using an optical microscope (Nikon Eclipse LV150 N) and a microhardness analysis was conducted on flat metallographic specimen across the joints using a Vicker’s micro-hardness testing machine (Make: Buehler’s Wilson; Model: VH1202) by applying 30 gm force for 10 sec dwell time.

In the case of actual busbar (1.5 mm 1060 Al) to prismatic cell terminal (4 mm 1060 Al) joint, only the lap shear and cross tension configurations were tested as the terminal material was thick and difficult to bend to form standard T-peel and torsion test specimens.

For AA1050 laser welds, the sheet metal parts representing the parent materials were simulated using shell elements, but it was found that solid brick elements offer greater accuracy in calculating stress in all three directions. As a result, a solid hexahedral mesh was employed to model the weld zone. Das et al. [21], Patil et al. [51] and Kumagai et al. [52], also considered shell elements for the sheet metal parts and solid elements for the welds when developing a weld model to save the run/computation time of the model without compromising the accuracy of the results. In order to capture the mechanical behaviour and failure modes of the laser-welded joint, a FE model was developed using the LS-DYNA platform [21,51,53,54]. In addition, researchers have also used the LS-DYNA platform for modelling other welding processes such as resistance spot welding and metal inert gas welding [55–57]. LS-DYNA is the industry leading multi-purpose explicit and implicit finite element and Multiphysics program used to analyse the nonlinear response of structures [58]. In addition, it is currently used by major automotive OEMs [59–62] as the software can simulate dynamic crash real events on full vehicles containing hundreds of components and millions of elements. This type of event is a highly non-linear explicit event. Material behaviour is governed by the definition of LS-DYNA material cards, the interaction between materials is governed via the definition of LS-DYNA contact cards, the stability of the models is supported by the use of LS-DYNA control cards [63] and governing equations of the explicit events are defined in the LS-DYNA code [64]. Current EV vehicles contain battery modules that contain mechanical joints such as laser welds. Solving time depends on the number of elements in the model and on the timestep being used. For large explicit models, large shell elements are used as their solving time is much lower than small solid elements. In some cases, when out of plane axial stresses are required to predict accurately the behaviour of the component solid elements are used. Hypermesh was used as a preprocessor to mesh the sheets (substrates) and the welds. Hypermesh is a common FE software used by major automotive OEMs [65]. However, there are some limitations of LS-DYNA FEA modelling. The main limitation when using any FEA code and in particular LS-DYNA is that the accuracy of FE predictions is mesh dependent. Highly integrated elements with a small size provide a more accurate response than low integrated big elements [66]. In addition, the main limitation in predicting joint strength is mesh size as the difference in stiffness and strength between the weld and the substrates generates a high stress concentration area on a small surface area. Big finite elements with a larger area than the area being highly stressed provide less accurate results.

In Fig. 5 , the model representations of the lap shear test specimens show where the rectangular seam was placed. The model includes a static grip, lower part, rectangular weld, upper part and moving part together with an elastic spring attached to the static grip to replicate the load cell behaviour during the experimental tests. The heat-affected zone (HAZ) around the weld was modelled with shell elements. Each sub-area of the specimen (upper part, lower part, HAZ, static grip and rigid grip) was modelled using elements having approximately constant sizes in order to avoid instability and large modelling influences on the calculation. To create the HAZ area within the upper and lower base materials, elements near the weld were chosen, the same method as applied by ELamouroux et al. [67] and Kumagai et al. [52]. Patil et al. [51] also used the same method to create a HAZ around the weld in the resistance spot weld model so that the material surrounding the weld nugget might fail according to the strain and strain rate that it experienced. In their experimental testing, there was a failure at the perimeter of the weld (HAZ).

In line with the experimental measurement, the sheets including static grip, moving grip, lower part and upper part were modelled with AA1050 material properties as given in Table 1. The four sheet metal parts (static grip, moving part, lower and upper part) were modelled using the standard Belytschko-Tsay membrane formulation [68]. An initial mesh sensitivity analysis was performed to determine the appropriate mesh size and it was observed that a square mesh size of 1 mm was suitable for sheet metal parts since a further reduction in element size did not have a significant impact on the results. For example, changing the mesh size from 1 mm to 0.75 mm resulted in a 0.1 % change in peak load. Similarly, the weld was modelled as a solid hexahedral mesh, however, a single layer of solid elements was not sufficient for obtaining a stable model due to excessive deformation and early element deletion. In order to overcome this issue, two (2 × 2) layers of solid elements, varying in size between 0.56 mm and 0.63 mm, were sufficient to mimic the behaviour of weld metal (i.e., load–displacement behaviour and an increase in layer number did not affect mechanical behaviour). Fig. 6 shows an enlarged view of the joint mesh as well as the side and section views of the lap share model. The model was made of 8,055 nodes, 4,080 shell elements and 104 solid elements. For placement of the weld between the upper and lower parts, two contact-based constraints were defined (i) between the upper part and the top surface of the weld zone and (ii) between the bottom surface of the weld zone and the lower part. The contacts were introduced by using LS-DYNA *TIED_SURFACE_TO_SURFACE contact treatment card. Due to the ease of placing the weld zone without changing the geometry or mesh, this modelling approach is useful for integrating the weld zone with other structural models. Thus, this approach can be applied to simulations of EV battery crashes and other structural integrity simulations. Models were developed for the static and moving grips based on rigid bodies, where the static grip was constrained to 6 degrees of freedom (i.e.,
U x
=
U y
=
U z
=
R x
=
R y
=
R z
= 0
) and the moving grip was displaced with a constant velocity profile in the direction of loading. All the LS-DYNA models were solved using explicit analysis. Therefore, it does not require an iterative solver for convergence. Each time step's solution is computed directly without iteration [69]. The angular velocity profile was considered for the torsion test model for rotating one of the rigid bodies. Three different material cards were used to simulate the base material and weld zone, including rigid bodies for static and moving/rotating grips, deformable bodies for the lower part, upper part and HAZ, and fusion zone cards for the weld zone. For the rigid body modelling, LS-DYNA material card *MAT_020: RIGID was used, while *MAT_024: Piecewise linear plasticity was used to simulate the material properties of the base deformable material and the weld fusion zone. The basic common material properties are listed in Table 1. By using a tabulated approach available in LS-DYNA, the stress–strain behaviour of the base material was incorporated into the material model, and an isotropic hardening effect was assumed for all materials. The properties of the base material were determined by mechanical testing while the characterisation of the fusion zone was more challenging, as it was difficult to obtain the properties of the fusion zone. However, it is possible to obtain information regarding the microhardness of the fusion zone. Consequently, the fusion zone hardening curve was derived using the base material and microhardness test results. In addition, fracture strain was also essential for obtaining the fracture within the required grip displacement. When the experimentally measured displacement-to-failure was reached, this fracture strain was selected to initiate fracture in the simulations.

Following satisfactory validation of AA1050 laser weld model, the same approach was adopted to develop a simple and efficient model for the actual laser weld joints between the busbar and cell terminal. As shown in Fig. 7 a, in order to simulate the laser joint (Fig. 1b), thick shell formulation 5 (Tshell-ELFORM = 5) was used for the terminal, while Belytschko-Tsay membrane formulation (thin shell) was used for the busbar parts. Considering that the bottom face of the terminal would be attached to the lid of the prismatic cell shown in Fig. 7b, a thick shell element was selected for the terminal since the mid-surface thin shell elements could be floated between the busbar and the lid. Also, the thick shell can account for stress distribution through the thickness of the shell, normal to the middle surface, and accommodate transverse shear deformations, which could be advantageous when developing a full battery pack model assembly [70]. Furthermore, a solid hexahedral mesh was used for the weld. This model contains 14,220 nodes, a 400 thick shell, 400 shell elements, and 1104 solid elements. The size of solid elements in weld varying between 0.37 mm and 0.50 mm, was adequate to simulate the behaviour of weld metal. By considering the integration of this model into the full battery pack model as shown in Fig. 7b, a relatively large square mesh of 2.5 mm was determined as the optimum size for the busbar and terminal sheets to obtain accurate results (i.e., to represent load–displacement behaviour) and reduce computational cost. In addition, to create a weld contact between the upper and lower sheets LS-DYNA *TIED_SURFACE_TO_SURFACE contact treatment card was used.
In this section, we report the experimental and simulation results of laser welded joints between AA1050, as well as the actual material used to join the busbar to the cell terminal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Laser welding and simulation of 1 mm to 1 mm AA1050

ce:section.ce:para: As discussed in section 2.2 and Table 2, the lap welded joints were prepared under varying levels of P considering three repetitions. Subsequently, average lap shear load (ALSL) was measured for sample no. S1, S2, S3 and S4 were 837.83 N, 1277.52 N, 1495.72 N and 1596.60 N, respectively. It can be seen that the ALSL increased as the P increased. The highest ALSL was obtained for sample no. S4 when the P was 950 W, whereas the lowest ALSL was obtained for sample S1 when the P was 700 W. This strength change with P can be explained by two important aspects of bead geometry, DOP and WW. The macro cross-sectional optical images of the weld bead of sample no. S1, S2, S3 and S4 are presented in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8 presents two geometrical characteristics of the fusion zone (namely, DOP and WW), which are often used to evaluate the quality of the joint. It has been found that a low P (700 W) gives insufficient DOP of the laser (128.23 µm) and a narrow WW (694.35 µm) which results in weak joints as a result of an under weld. The joint became better with the incremental increase in P, resulting in a higher failure load of the joint. With 950 W P, the maximum ALSL was measured at 1596.60 N due to the deeper DOP (692.32 µm) and the wider WW (1277.32 µm) of the keyhole. Weld quality was good for the maximum load sample no. S4 with no observable defects on the top surface. The fracture mode from lap shear tests is shown in Fig. 9 . Interfacial failure was observed for sample no. S1, S2 and S3, while for S4 the failure was initiated at the circumference of the laser weld (i.e., it could be from the heat-affected zone, which is the weakest zone, into the laser weld) and propagated by tearing the base material. Due to the strength of the weld nugget, it was able to remain intact with failure occurring in both the lower and upper sheets. The results indicate that 950 W P, 2000 mm/min S, 600 Hz Wf and 0.5 mm WA are optimal parameters for achieving high-quality welds and can be utilized to prepare samples for T-peel, cross-tension and torsion tests.

As welding changes, the mechanical properties of the material in the fusion zone, a microhardness analysis was also conducted to determine the microhardness of the parent material and the fusion zone. As discussed earlier, obtaining microhardness values can be relatively straightforward, although mapping the mechanical properties of the fusion zone can be challenging. This test was conducted for maximum load samples (S4) in order to evaluate the microhardness profile of the lap joint. There was a total of 28 indents distributed over the upper and lower material sheets (i.e., 17 indents in the upper and 11 indents in the lower material sheets) and they were kept at an equal distance of 0.2 mm apart as shown in Fig. 10 . Each hardness profile (upper sheet and lower sheet) traverses the fusion from base material to base material. Three distinct sub-zones, fusion zone, heat-affected zone and base metal, as previously suggested a clearly identified. Microhardness was repeated in three separate sections to guarantee reliable data. Microhardness profiles of top and bottom base metal (BM) sheets are almost flat, consistent uniform microstructures. The laser weld can also be characterized as a homogenous region since the fusion zones in the upper and lower sheets exhibit a constant microhardness. In contrast, the heat-affected zone can be described as a heterogeneous area as the microhardness changes dramatically within a short distance. The hardness value of the upper and lower base metal was recorded in the range of 26.2–26.8 HV0.03 with an average hardness of 26.4 HV0.03. Microhardness of the fusion zone (FZ) varies from 27.0 to 27.9 HV0.03 with an average hardness of 27.5 HV0.03. It can be seen that the microhardness increased slightly (by up to 4.2 %) in the FZ in comparison with the parent metals. Similarly, the hardness of the HAZ is slightly lower than the base metal hardness varying from 24.6 to 24.9 HV0.03 with an average hardness of 24.7 HV0.03. In both the upper and lower layers, a consistent trend was observed in the fusion zones. As the FZ was formed from a mixture of two identical base metal structures, no filler materials were added, and due to the regular distributions of precipitates, a homogeneous microstructure was achieved. In general, the microhardness of a welded joint depends on the metallurgical condition of the weld material and the possible presence of defects [5,71]. Das et al. [72] and Kumar et al. [5] achieved a similar trend of microstructure profile for the Al alloy. Furthermore, a few studies were reported where grain orientation has a significant effect on the microhardness [73,74] and it is evident from Fig. 10 that the weld microstructures were different as compared to the base materials. Therefore, the change in microhardness in the fusion zone may be attributed to the grain orientation in the FZ [5]. Overall, it was observed that the microhardness was increased by 4 % in the fusion zone compared to base metal. Using these changes in microhardness values, the mechanical properties of sub-areas could be defined [67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modelling of laser joints and failure prediction
ce:section.ce:para:
As described in section 3, the base material properties were obtained from the manufacturer as well as by performing tensile tests according to standards shown in Fig. 3. Thus, the density, Young's modulus and Poisson's ratio for the upper and lower base materials were set at 2710
kg
m
3
, 58,000
MPa
, and 0.33, respectively. The hardening curves for the HAZ and FZ were created based on the hardness change of the base materials using the relationship
σ
HAZ / F Z
=
σ
BM
× (
HV
HAZ / F Z
HV
BM
) [21,67,75]. The FZ is composed of both the top and bottom base materials which are the same. In comparison with the base metal, HAZ and FZ microhardness values were 0.93 times lower and 1.04 times higher, respectively. The hardening curves for base material, HAZ and FZ are shown in Fig. 11 . Based on the experimental lap shear test, the fracture strain was selected to initiate the fracture in the simulation at a grip displacement. Additional material data and model-specific details are listed in Table 3 . The same material properties were used for modelling other joint configurations.

Sample no. S4 was selected for the development of the lap shear finite element model since the average maximum ALSL, and the base metal failure were obtained from this sample. One load–displacement curve was selected for validation of the developed model from three test results obtained for sample no. S4. The results of computing the lap shear model using the mechanical properties derived from each sub-area (fusion zone, HAZ zone and base metal) in the LS-DYNA software, are shown in Fig. 12 . Fig. 12a shows that the load-grip displacement curve obtained from the simulation and experimental were similar. The maximum load obtained from the lap shear tensile test and simulation test was 1623.09 N and 1637.80 N, respectively. Images from the deformation sequence at specific grip displacements are shown in Fig. 12b. It can be seen that the maximum load obtained from the simulation was at a grip displacement of 1.68 mm and failure was initiated from the circumference of the weld at grip displacement of 5 mm and then propagated by tearing of both the upper and lower base metal at a grip displacement of 10 mm. According to Fig. 12c, the failure mode obtained from the simulation matches the experimental data for the given load condition (i.e., at a grip displacement of 10 mm). Considering the microhardness profile, the failure must have occurred in the HAZ since the zone is softer than the weld and base metal. In order to derive the maximum load from a lap shear model, fracture strain was one of the most important factors to consider. A fracture strain-based sensitivity analysis of lap shear strength was performed, and an appropriate fracture strain was selected to initiate fracture at the desired load for the upper and lower HAZs of the material.

Figs. 13 and 14
illustrate the load–displacement curves resulting from T-peel and cross-tension tests, as well as the corresponding simulation model. The maximum T-peel strengths obtained from the simulation and test were 710.15 N and 696.87 N, respectively. Whereas the maximum cross-tension strength obtained from the simulation and test were 1048.38 N and 1031.96 N, respectively. Thus, in both cases, the difference between simulations and experimentations was only a small percentage, which validated the developed model. As can also be seen from the load–displacement curves (Fig. 13a and Fig. 14a), the modelling of the laser weld was very accurate. Similar failure modes for both the cross-tension and T-peel tests were observed as shown in Fig. 13b and 14b, respectively. Selected images from the deformation sequence at specific grip displacements for the T-peel and cross-tension tests are shown in Fig. 13c and Fig. 14c, respectively. Failure in the top sheet around the circumference of the weld (mainly HAZ) and slowly propagated through the base material. Due to the fact that the weld was attached to the lower base material and the failure occurred in the upper base material, the quality of the weld was classified as a good weld.

It was also tested whether the detailed model could accurately represent torsion samples, which has generally been overlooked by researchers in their weld models [21,51,52,54,67,70,75]. The torsion test results and simulations are given in Fig. 15 . In this case, the torque-rotation angle was recorded and validated with experimental results. As can be seen in Fig. 15b, the fracture surface occurred in the weld due to the maximum stress applied to it due to the torsion test. Subsequently, the fracture strain was varied for the weld material as a fracture was obtained in the weld part, and the appropriate fracture strain value was determined. Again, using a strain-based sensitivity analysis an appropriate fracture strain for the weld material was determined. The subsequent simulations of the torque-rotation angle curves (Fig. 15a) were in good agreement with the actual test results. Selected images from the deformation sequence at a specific grip rotation angle for the torsion test are shown in Fig. 15c. The results indicate that the numerical models of lap shear, T-peel, cross-tension and torsion specimens agree well with experimental results for a given 1 mm Al 1050 material under quasi-static loading conditions. The developed modelling approach was therefore used to model the actual laser joints between the busbar and prismatic cell terminal (Fig. 7b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation and failure prediction for busbar to cell terminal joint strength
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the joint strength obtained in the pilot study and using some additional optimization experiments and their results, the P of 2500 W, S 34.2 mm/s of was used for welding busbar-to-cell terminal joint. A pictorial view of the unwelded prismatic cell is shown in Fig. 16 a and the welded cell is shown in Fig. 16b showing two busbars successfully welded.

Fig. 17 a shows the representative weld of a busbar and cell terminal which was used to validate the simulation model. In order to replicate real weld conditions, the same parameters and weld geometry used for welding the busbar and cell terminal coupon material were used for welding the actual busbar and cell terminal (Fig. 16b). Fig. 17b shows the failure mode obtained for the three repetitions of the lap shear tests. Fracture was observed to occur in the upper sheet material near the circumference of the weld and the weld bead was strong enough to remain intact with the lower sheet. The average peak load, for the three tests was approximately 4650 N. These weld strengths are acceptable since the tensile strength of 1060 Al alone is approximately 2450 N and 6304 N for 1.5 mm and 4 mm Al, respectively. Fig. 18 displays an optical image of the weld fusion zone, with the yellow dashed line representing the boundary between the fusion zone and the base material. The weld micrographs show a few voids which could be caused by absorption and entrapment of the gases, collapse of the keyhole, alloy elements vaporization, etc [22,76–78], and illustrate the importance of optimizing welding parameters in order to minimize defects. As a whole, the weld surface appearance was good without any undercuts or overcuts to the base metal. There was also approximately 17 % penetration of the bottom sheet. Full penetration is not ideal for battery applications, particularly in busbar to terminal welds as this may cause cracks to form on the lid of prismatic cells, resulting in electrolyte leakage or possible thermal runaway. This emphasises the importance of controlling penetration depth for low thermal input. Therefore, the laser welds obtained using the optimized parameters for the lap shear samples were considered of good quality and further cross-tension testing was conducted. In addition, Fig. 19 illustrates the microhardness profile of the weld, where hardness was measured at the upper, middle and lower portions of the weld cross-section. In both the base metal and fusion zone areas, microhardness values are consistent. Microhardness for the FZ varies from 37.5 to 40.6 HV0.03 with an average of 39.2 HV0.03 decreased slightly in comparison to lower (average microhardness ∼ 41.4 HV0.03) and upper (average microhardness ∼ 46.3 HV0.03) base metal by 5 % and 15 %, respectively. Microhardness for the HAZ varied from 33.5 to 34.9 HV0.03 with an average of 34.1 HV0.03 decreased slightly (by up to 13 %) in comparison to FZ. This microhardness variation profile was used to derive the plastic flow curve for fusion and HAZ areas for the simulation model. The plastic flow curve for the upper sheet was selected to represent the weld area, since the fusion zone was mostly composed of upper material and failure occurred within the upper portion of the weld. The HAZ plastic flow curve was derived based on the corresponding base metal in the given area.

The material properties like density, Young's modulus and Poisson's ratio for the 1.5 mm 1060 Al were set at 2710
kg
m
3
, 59,000
MPa
, and 0.36, respectively. Whereas, for 4 mm 1060 Al the density, Young's modulus and Poisson's ratio for the 1.5 mm 1060 Al was set at 2710
kg
m
3
, 61,000
MPa
, and 0.38, respectively. The results obtained from the lap shear simulation are given in Fig. 20 . The maximum load obtained from the simulation and actual tests was 4612 N and 4654 N, respectively, which is in line with expectations. Fig. 20a shows that the load–displacement curve obtained from the experimentation and simulation tests are similar. The images from the deformation sequence at specific grip displacement are shown in Fig. 20b. The failure modes from the experimental and simulation are given in Fig. 20c, where the failure was initiated close to the weld and propagated through the base metal. As with lap share, Fig. 21 illustrates the curve of load–displacement obtained from the cross-tension test and the corresponding simulation model. In the simulation tests, a maximum cross-tension load of 3796 N was obtained, which was 100 N or approximately 2.5 % less than the load obtained in the experimental test (3893 N). From the load–displacement curves (Fig. 21a), it can also be seen that the modelling of the laser weld is in good agreement with the actual cross-tension test. Fig. 21b shows the failure modes obtained from the simulation and experiment test, where the failure was initiated from the upper sheet and the weld was strong enough to remain intact with the lower sheet. According to the results, the model produced a load prediction that was close to the actual load for the busbar to cell terminal test. The data provided by these simulations validates the use of the modelling methodology and provided an FE model which could be incorporated into a multi-cell module to predict deformation under mechanical abuse.

A modelling methodology was developed in this study to represent laser welds used in EV battery applications. Weld properties were characterised using 1 mm to 1 mm AA1050 in lap, T-peel, cross-tension and torsion configurations. The process was then applied to develop a simplified model for busbar to prismatic cell terminal laser joints that could be used in full vehicle simulations. From the foregoing analysis and discussion, the following conclusions were drawn:
•
Modelling of laser weld predicts similar load–displacement and torque-rotation angle behaviour to that obtained in a physical test.
•
The models can be used for predicting the responses (i.e., load and torque) and failure in a computationally efficient way.
•
The percentage error between the LS-DYNA modelled and the experimental results are found to be approximately ∼ 2.5 %, which validates the applied modelling approach.
•
For confirming the quality of the weld, joint fusion zone characterizations were conducted, followed by a simple Vickers hardness test in order to determine the mechanical properties (key flow stress behaviour) of the fusion zone and heat-affected zone.

The results of this study were verified by experimental and computational analysis, and it was concluded that modelling was a suitable method of predicting joint strength at an early stage. For the battery module to behave accurately under abusive loading conditions, further work is required to integrate the model into a full battery module or pack. In addition, XRD and EBSD analyses will be conducted for the welds for better understating of grain formation.
